clean_story: Simple rules.  Surprising outcomes. "Emergent behaviors" describe patterns that appear when many small things follow basic instructions—like ants building bridges or birds forming flocks, without a leader. Imagine shaking a box of marbles. —Each one moves randomly, yet together they settle into layers. No marble planned it. Traffic jams emerge this way. Drivers brake to avoid collisions—simple rule. But enough cars reacting creates a wave of stopped traffic, miles long. No single driver caused it. Fireflies sync their flashes in forests. Each adjusts its rhythm slightly to match neighbors. Soon, thousands pulse in unison—no conductor. Even slime molds, brainless blobs, solve mazes by growing toward food while avoiding light. Their "decisions" come from cells competing for resources. Three related subjects are swarm intelligence, self-organizing systems and cellular automata.
